General comment - We shall not have this file here.
The Common Schema is stored in DCAE project, and from there it is fetched to a DCAE/VES collector Dockers, when these are built.

Having the schema separately in the doc means, that each update we need to do twice. What storing the schema in DCAEGEN2 project directly, and maybe we could refer to that in the DOC?

All schemas are available in human-friendly form here:
https://github.com/onap/dcaegen2-collectors-ves/tree/master/etc

This is a little unclear to me.
As far as I recall, the startEpochMicrosec is a timestamp, when the 1st event related to the fault was reported.

When other events related to that fault are received, they are expected to have the same startEpochMicrosec, but the lastEpochMicrosec is updated.

This sentence "when that event is generated" - it is not actually when the event is generated, as we have multiple events related to a single fault. "when this fault is first reported" sounds to be better, at least to me.
Following the comment before... "when the fault is raised"...

Well, We need to be careful about this in a specification like this one here. We need to provide a single solution here, and not possible alternatives. What does that mean for the NF providers? That they could use any approach mentioned here?
Or they should use the 1st one, and be informed about future changes?

We have other protocols/serialization methods used in other collectors (e.g. HV-VES)

VES seems to be simply REST/JSON.

https://192.168.0.101:8080/eventListener/v7

We will need the path anyway, as there might be different versions of VES running, and there are 2 VES endpoints (eventListener and eventBatch).
This is a VES specific behavior, that can stay here - but most probably in VES Collector configuration section.
To me, this sounds like a VNF Requirement.
Again, that`s a VNF Requirement, not a VES Event Listener characteristic.

If this applies to 3GPP devices, then there is a dedicated PM job definition within 3GPP specs, which has relevant property.

If not - this shall go to VNF Requirements, and maybe there we can mention that requirement for non-3GPP NFs.

(For 3GPP NFs, that requirement is covered in 3GPP specs. Perhaps we coudl note, which 3GPP spec that 3GPP_compliant box needs to be compatible with)

I don't understand this example notification3gppPerfFileReady0005.  I would have thought that it would be the domain notification0005.  There is not currently a notification called 3gppPerfFileReady to the best of my knowledge.  there is a fileReady notification type. Are we saying that if there are different subtypes within a domain like notification then we should be including the type in the event ID? I think if this is the case then all the types within a domain need to also be included

this should say is the timestamp when the original onset of the fault occurred.  The timestamp for the clear should also be the timestamp for the onset of the original fault.  The starEpochMicrosec for a clear should not be when the clear arrives.
I agree the specification should be saying what is expected not what might be done.  This leaves it fully open to interpretation.  I have no idea what VES requires

Shouldn't this also go in the VNF requirements?  Are we saying that a NF needs to be able to support a different destination IP address for different domains if the service provider wishes to request this?  That is really the requirement and its not on the VES Event Listener.  If a NF sends an event to a collector and it is not valid then the collector should discard it.  So if the service provider has their network set up such that they have configured different collector FQDNS/IP Addresses then the collector should reject the event.  It should be a VNF requirement on the NF to be able to support multiple destinations.  This would go in one of the general requirements sections I think.

I won't repeat myself I agree with Damian on his remarks in this section regarding what is appropriate for the Event Listener chapter and what should go in other chapters of the VNF requirements.  We can help direct what other chapters might be appropriate.

isn't this in conflict with some of the above statements.  I thought that the NF had to be able to support different listeners for different domains.  This is implying that no you only have to support one listener.  I think we have to sort out what this is for.  I am thinking that if it is related to the other general requirements for what a NF must support then it doesn't belong in this chapter.
this is an example of a requirement that I think should be in the VNF requirements general section but is okay to have here as well.
shouldn't it just say send?  Streaming implies a websocket or persistent connection

How does one identify these batch events do they have to all be from the same domain?  I don't understand how one populates headers etc to indicate that this is a batch.  Will the Collector look at each event individually?  How do these events get to the proper topic if they are from different domains.

There were some email discussions last year about eventId, with conclusion that format domain<nnn> is not mandatory. It is just an example. One of the things that triggered this dicussion is that the notification domain can be used for several purposes and it may not be practical to have a single eventId sequence. I don't agree to adding new rules about value of eventId, maybe it needs to be clarified what is just example and what is requirement.
I think this line can be removed. There is a more complete description of the VES Event Listener URL already in section 'Resource Structure'.

Buffering is also described elsewhere in the spec and the requirements are not fully consistent.  This text could be interpreted that all events must be buffered which is not consistent with separate section regarding buffering. Also here it says at least 1h while other section says max 1h.

We should not require a certain number of digits in the number, so examples heartbeat3, notification11 should be valid

This should be removed. This active/standby behavior for FQDNs or IP addresses is not supported by 3GPP Subscription Control NRM.  If a destination address subscribes to an event, events go to that destination.  If 2 destinations subscribe, events go to 2 destinations.  There is no concept of try one first, then the other.

This should be removed.  First - It is unclear.  NF doesn't have an event listener, ONAP does.  Second - If this means NF should have 2 destination addresses for each event listener; active and standby - this is not supported by 3GPP Subscription NRM.  3GPP NF sends to all destinations that subscribe.
Remove. Active/standby destination addresses are not supported in 3GPP Subscription NRM.
Agree

I don't agree with this proposal. The authoritative version of the JSON Schema should be published and owned by the VNF requirements project. In case it is kept in DCAE, it creates doubts about spec ownership and increased risk for divergence.
I understand that DCAE is one of the primary users of this schema, and that importing new versions should be as easy as possible. But I think discussion should start with looking at how to simplify that process.

I agree that requirements for the VNF/PNF that are not directly related to the VES protocol definition should generally go into VNF requirements (section 7.4 Monitoring & Management). There are already several examples of such VES-related requirements today in 7.4. Some of them also overlap with requirements below while not always being fully aligned, in those cases further analysis and discussion is needed to resolve the overlap or misalignment.

For this review, it seems most of the proposed new text in the following sections would fall into this category and would fit better in VNF requirements (7.4):
* Configuration Requirements
* Event Domain Requirements/Expectations
* Use of Collector FQDNs and/or IP Address
* (Parts of) Buffering, e g what events to buffer and for how long

(Conversely, probably some of the existing text in 7.4 should be removed in the future, since it repeats parts of the protocol description from this document.)
I agree that implementations might use a more sophisticated approach.

In the 7.1.1 draft-4 Word document, there is review comment explaining that the second part with sending of fault event in this scenario is optional. As those extra review comments are not imported here, I think the text above needs to be clarified to make it clear this is not mandatory.
I think this looks like part of the protocol definition. (In this case it is really re-stating content of RFC2617).
I don't agree that official JSON Schema should be kept in the DCAE project. See separate comment for the Schema file review.

I would have a suggestion here.
heartBeat shall be allowed for all the endPoints.

It is not only used to check, if an NF is alive from ONAP perspective, it can be used as well as a keep-alive mechanism for xNF, against a VES endpoint. That`s why I think HeartBeat shall be allowed for each VES Endpoint, or... a different mechanism needs to be proposed, so an NF is able to periodically check the health of each configured VES event Listener endpoint.

Oskar, I think, it should be kept in 1 place only.
Now, we have 2 copies - one in DCAE, nad one in VNF-Rqts.

I am afraid, the two files will get de-synchronized really soon. Actually, it doesn`t matter, where this schema is located, if there is only one copy. That`s the ultimate goal.

Or shall it work like this for all events but faults?

Initially, it was 1,5 MB, then 2 MB, now going back to 1 MB.
Extending is backwards compatible (1,5 -> 2 MBs).
Shrinking is not backwards compatible (2MB -> 1MB). I might have an implementation based on 2MB limit, it won`t work with 1MB limit, we introduce here.
See coment below. This is not a backwards compatible change.

I don`t understand this part:
1. "The NF must create an internal flag....." Sounds like a VNF requirement for me.

2. I don`t treally understand the purpose. Say I sand an event to VES, I`ll get HTTP/503 internal server error. It doesn`t mean, that the event I was sending is wrong in any way. 
Why shall I fix these events as a vendor? Events are fine (I can validate them locally against a schema) - but teh receiving end might be wrong (e.g. no more disk space, no more memory, no networking to DMaaP - this will all result in 5xx HTTP response code).
Would be nice to correct this to a real value, here and in the previous examples.
Not a backwards compatible change (2->1MB).

I'm copying the entire email below for reference. It was sent to several people that are also reviewers here on June 14, 2019. I got agreement from Nokia, while nobody objected:

>You agree that we can have different eventIDs for different notification file types but you don’t want to specify the exact format of the eventID; e.g. notification.<changeIdnetifier>.x.

Yes

>It is enough to say that the eventId must uniquely identify a sequence of event types from a particular source.

It is important to be clear what is meant by source. Within the common header there is a sourceName field, and our interpretation of the VES specification is that the only MUST requirement for eventIDs is that if sourceName is the same, then eventIDs must be unique (except special case where sequence field is used as well). In addition to this, you could perhaps talk about even more fine-grained (logical) event sources such as producers of different kind of files. But whether those logical sources share the same eventID sequence or not would be up to the implementation.

>It is up to the NF to craft an eventId that identifies the source and is a monotonously increasing integer that lets the manager determine when a particular type of event is missing.

Creating eventID using some prefix followed by monotonously increasing integer could be described as recommended format. However I think that would be at most a SHOULD requirement, as otherwise existing NFs may no longer be compliant with the spec. Also how many prefixes and corresponding eventID sequences to use per domain would be implementation-specific as discussed above.

>Also leading zeroes are not required.

I expect this is the case already today, even if some of the examples in the VES spec include leading zeroes.
